summary: On Sunday, Sen. Thom Tillis of North Carolina dropped his opposition to President Donald Trump's Federal Reserve nominee, Kevin Warsh, removing a major hurdle for the GOP-controlled Senate Banking Committee to advance the nomination.

The Justice Department ended its investigation into the Federal Reserve's $2.5 billion building renovations, which had stalled Warsh's confirmation process. Tillis received assurances the case was completely settled, enabling him to move forward.

Scheduled for Wednesday, the committee will vote on Warsh's nomination after he pledged at a hearing last week to be "an independent actor." President Trump said Saturday he expects the process to be smooth.

Massachusetts Sen. Elizabeth Warren denounced the nomination, arguing Warsh "proved in his nomination hearing to be nothing more than President Trump's sock puppet" and questioned his ability to lead the Fed independently.

Since appointing Jerome Powell in 2017, President Trump has pressured the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates. Even if replaced, Powell could elect to remain on the board as a governor until January 2028.
title: Source Icon

summary: FBI agents investigated a Torrance, California address Sunday connected to Cole Tomas Allen, identified as the suspect in Saturday night's shooting at the White House Correspondents Dinner attended by President Donald Trump .

In writings sent to family members minutes before the attack, the 31-year-old described himself as 'Friendly Federal Assassin' and expressed opposition to recent government actions under Trump, according to a law enforcement official.

Allen earned a bachelor's degree in mechanical engineering from the California Institute of Technology in Pasadena and a master's degree in computer science from California State University, Dominguez Hills, working six years at C2 Education.

Court records show no prior criminal history for Allen, who contributed $25 to a Democratic Party political action committee supporting Kamala Harris in 2024, while his father Thomas Allen serves as an elder at Grace United Reformed Church Torrance.

Bin Tang, a computer science professor at California State University, Dominguez Hills, taught Allen before his graduation last year and described him as 'very polite, a good fellow,' expressing shock upon learning of his arrest.
title: Cole Thomas Allen Identified as Suspect in White House Correspondents’ Dinner Shooting

summary: On Saturday, an armed suspect opened fire at The Washington Hilton during The White House Correspondents dinner, prompting Secret Service agents to swiftly evacuate President Donald Trump .
Cole Tomas Allen, 31, of Torrance, California, allegedly carried a shotgun and handgun into the hotel and faces charges including assaulting an officer with a dangerous weapon.

One officer was shot but remained protected by a bulletproof vest, while President Trump praised the Secret Service's quick response, calling the incident 'shocking' and the suspect a 'lone wolf whack job.'
Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche promised 'justice will be served,' while Washington Mayor Muriel Bowser stated there is 'no reason' to believe any other individuals were involved.
Trump vowed to reschedule the event within 30 days, as The Washington Hilton previously saw security tightened after an attempted assassination of President Ronald Reagan in 1981.
title: Trump Evacuated After Shots Fired at White House Correspondents' Dinner

summary: On Saturday, Secret Service agents evacuated President Donald Trump , First Lady Melania Trump, and Vice President JD Vance from the White House Correspondents' Association Dinner at the Washington Hilton after gunshots were heard near the ballroom.

This event marked Trump's first attendance as president, highlighting his contentious relationship with the press. The Washington Hilton ballroom also served as the site of the 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan.
Secret Service spokesperson Anthony Guglielmi confirmed one suspect is in custody. Trump and all other protectees are safe with no immediate reports of injuries among attendees.

White House Correspondents' Association President Weijia Jiang announced the event will be postponed and rescheduled within 30 days. Organizers are cooperating with law enforcement as they assess the incident.

The FBI Washington field office is assisting in the ongoing investigation as authorities sweep the venue to assess security protocols. Reviews of screening procedures and access controls are expected to follow.
title: Trump safe after shooting at White House Correspondents' Dinner, suspect in custody

Right-Leaning coverage counters by emphasizing Iran's "regime" "infighting," "confusion," and lack of leadership—"nobody knows who is in charge"—portraying the move as decisive strength with "all the cards.

summary: On Saturday, President Donald Trump abruptly canceled a planned trip to Islamabad, Pakistan, for Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and senior adviser Jared Kushner to conduct indirect peace talks with Iran.

Trump cited "too much time wasted on traveling, too much work" and internal Iranian "infighting" for the decision, after Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi departed Pakistan Saturday without meeting U.S. officials.

Within 10 minutes of canceling the trip, Trump claimed Iran submitted a proposal that "could have been better," yet rejected it, as the U.S. maintains a naval blockade on Iranian ports which Iran characterizes as "banditry and piracy" in the Strait of Hormuz.

Araghchi signaled skepticism regarding Washington's intentions, stating, "Have yet to see if the U.S. is truly serious about diplomacy," leaving direct U.S.-Iran engagement stalled while ceasefire discussions rely on Pakistani mediation.

While the indefinite ceasefire pauses most fighting, the economic fallout persists as energy shipments face disruption, though Trump indicated the door remains open, stating, "If they want to talk, all they have to do is call!!!".
title: Trump cancels US delegation’s Pakistan trip as Iran peace talks stall

summary: On Saturday, Cole Tomas Allen, 31, charged a security checkpoint at the Washington Hilton during the White House Correspondents' Dinner, firing a shotgun and wounding a Secret Service agent before being tackled and arrested.

Sent to his family 10 minutes before the attack, Allen's manifesto dubbed him the "Friendly Federal Assassin" and detailed plans to kill Trump administration officials, according to law enforcement officials.
Allen legally purchased a.38-caliber pistol in October 2023 and a 12-gauge shotgun in August 2025, then traveled from Southern California to Washington, D.C. by rail to execute the attack.

President Donald Trump and other officials were safely evacuated as security personnel formed a protective cordon; the wounded Secret Service agent escaped serious injury because his bulletproof vest stopped the shot.
Trump intends to reschedule the dinner within 30 days while continuing to lobby for a secure ballroom facility on White House grounds to prevent future security lapses at high-profile events.
title: Trump says Correspondents' dinner shooting suspect wrote anti-Christian manifesto

summary: On Sunday, House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries said Democrats will not prioritize impeaching President Trump if they regain the House majority, answering "Of course not" when asked.
Trump warned House Republicans in January that he would be impeached if the party lost the midterms, though removal efforts emerged earlier this year and have largely stalled.
Jeffries emphasized the party's top priority is driving down the high cost of living, advocating for good-paying jobs, housing, healthcare, education, and retirement security for Americans.
Rep. Greg Meeks said Democrats must secure votes to achieve legislative goals, adding his efforts will focus on the American economy and people rather than impeachment.
Democrats are leveraging redistricting efforts nationwide to capture additional House seats and regain working-class voter support following losses in the 2024 election.
title: Jeffries Says Democrats Will Focus on Affordability, Not Trump Impeachment

summary: A recent U.S. military strike in the eastern Pacific Ocean killed three people on a boat accused of drug trafficking, according to U.S. Southern Command's social media post.
Since early September, a campaign to attack alleged drug-trafficking vessels in Latin American waters has resulted in at least 186 deaths.
The U.S. military has not provided evidence that the targeted vessels were carrying drugs but said it targeted traffickers along known smuggling routes.
The military campaign coincides with increased U.S. military activity in the region amid concerns about drug trafficking.
title: Latest US military strike on alleged drug boat kills 3 in eastern Pacific

summary: On Saturday, April 25, 2026, gunfire erupted outside the ballroom at the Washington Hilton during the White House Correspondents Association dinner, forcing the rapid evacuation of President Donald Trump , First Lady Melania Trump, and other officials.

Authorities arrested 31-year-old Cole Tomas Allen of Torrance, California, after he bypassed security checkpoints armed with multiple weapons. Officials described Allen as appearing to act alone, though some intelligence figures have publicly questioned that narrative.
One Secret Service agent was struck by a bullet but survived thanks to his bulletproof vest. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche announced that charges would be filed "shortly" against the suspect.

President Trump struck an unusually conciliatory tone during an impromptu briefing, calling on Americans to "recommit" and resolve their differences peacefully after the incident. He noted the dinner was intended to unite members of both parties.

This marks the third assassination attempt on Trump since July 2024, following incidents in Butler, Pennsylvania, and West Palm Beach, Florida. Trump suggested that "the most impactful people" face unique threats, framing the pattern as a sign of his presidency's success.
title: Trump calls for unity and bipartisan healing after another violent incident

summary: Donald Trump ’s Justice Department is reportedly using the recent shooting incident at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner to pressure preservationists over a legal dispute.
The dispute concerns a planned $400 million ballroom project at the site of the former East Wing of the White House.
Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche publicly urged that construction should proceed, calling for the lawsuit to be dropped.
Assistant Attorney General Brett Shumate gave the National Trust for Historic Preservation a deadline to withdraw its lawsuit, which seeks to block the project.
title: Justice Department Presses Preservationists to Drop Trump Ballroom Lawsuit After Shooting

summary: Global military spending reached a record $2.887 trillion in 2025, marking the 11th consecutive year of growth, according to new data from the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute .

Fueled by the ongoing war in Ukraine, European military budgets spiked 14% to $864 billion, researcher Xiao Liang said. Spending focus shifted toward Central and Western European countries as militarization plans materialized.
The United States remained the largest spender at $954 billion, though expenditures fell 7.5% because Congress approved no new financial military aid for Ukraine in 2025.
Rising expenditures pushed the global military burden to 2.5% of worldwide GDP, the highest level since 2009, as nations compensated for increased insecurity.

Following Congress approval of a 2026 budget signaling increased military spending, the upward trend likely persists through 2026 and beyond, researcher Lorenzo Scarazzato noted, with potential for further rises if President Donald Trump's 2027 proposal passes.
title: Global military spending rises 2.9% despite US decline over Ukraine freeze

summary: President Donald Trump criticized CBS reporter Norah O'Donnell during a "60 Minutes" interview for reading excerpts from the alleged manifesto of the White House Correspondents' Dinner shooting suspect, calling her 'a disgrace' and denying the accusations in the manifesto.
The suspect, Cole Allen, fired shots at the Washington Hilton during the event, injuring a Secret Service officer, and was apprehended at the scene.
Federal investigators are reviewing the suspect's writings as part of their ongoing investigation, while the White House Correspondents' Association postponed the event due to security concerns.
Authorities have charged Cole Allen with using a firearm during a crime of violence and assaulting a federal officer.
title: Trump criticizes “60 Minutes” host over on-air reading of suspect’s alleged manifesto

summary: Prime Minister Netanyahu explicitly labeled the shooting an "assassination attempt" and expressed deep shock over the repeated threats against the President and First Lady .
President Isaac Herzog offered prayers "from the Holy Land" for the safety of the United States and its leadership, describing the event as a "heinous attack" on democratic stability.
Both leaders formally saluted the U.S. Secret Service for their "swift and decisive action" in tackling the gunman before he could enter the main ballroom.
The Israeli leadership sent specific wishes for a "full and speedy recovery" to the police officer who was shot and injured during the confrontation in the hotel lobby.
title: Israeli PM Netanyahu, President Herzog Condemn Assassination Attempt on Trump

summary: A 14-year-old boy wanted in a deadly Ontario mall shooting turned himself in to Hamilton Police on Sunday, ending the search for the suspect.
The shooting occurred Friday inside Jackson Square mall in Hamilton, where the 16-year-old victim walked toward a store and collapsed after being shot.
Investigators determined the teens interacted outside Jackson Square before an altercation inside led to the shooting; police have now spoken to everyone involved and seek no further suspects.
Chief Frank Bergen called the incident a "community call to action," stating that it is deeply troubling another young person is alleged to be responsible.
Investigators continue to appeal to anyone who may have witnessed the incident to contact police, with details first reported by The Canadian Press on April 26, 2026.
title: 14-Year-Old Surrenders in Hamilton Mall Shooting That Killed Teen

summary: Former Rep. Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick is continuing her reelection campaign despite resigning from Congress on Tuesday, four days after filing her candidacy paperwork ahead of an expected expulsion vote.

The House Ethics Committee found Cherfilus-McCormick responsible for 25 ethics violations, alleging she improperly funneled $5 million in federal funds intended for COVID-19 programs; she faces up to 53 years in prison.

Cherfilus-McCormick has pleaded not guilty, maintaining that the House Ethics Committee hearing was "not a fair process" and a "witch hunt" as the investigation involved dozens of subpoenas and over 33,000 documents.
Florida Democratic Party Chair Nikki Fried condemned the resignation, stating that "corruption has no place in Congress" after Cherfilus-McCormick was indicted for misusing COVID-19 funds.

At least five other Democrats are vying for the Florida 20th congressional district seat, a field that includes educator Elijah Manley and Luther "Uncle Luke" Campbell from the 1990s rap group 2 Live Crew.
title: Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick Files for Re-Election While Keeping FEC Accounts Active

summary: CNN host Dana Bash faced criticism on Sunday for questioning Representative Jamie Raskin about Democrats' "heated rhetoric" toward President Donald Trump following a shooting outside the White House Correspondents' Association dinner.
Bash suggested divisive rhetoric intersected with a spike in political violence in recent years, pressing Raskin on the party's characterization of Trump as "terrible for this country."

Raskin responded by asking "What rhetoric?" and clarified he has no "personal problem" with Trump, emphasizing his focus on the administration's "authoritarianism" during the Minneapolis immigration crackdown.
title: Rep. Jamie Raskin questioned about Democratic rhetoric toward Trump after White House Correspondents’ Dinner shooting

summary: On Saturday, April 25, 2026, a gunman opened fire at the Washington Hilton during the White House Correspondents' Association dinner, prompting Secret Service agents to evacuate President Donald Trump and lock down the venue.

Authorities identified the suspect as 31-year-old Cole Tomas Allen of Torrance, California, who traveled by train from Los Angeles to Washington, D.C. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche stated officials believe Allen "was targeting administration officials."

During the exchange with security, one officer was shot from close range but survived, saved by a bulletproof vest. Trump confirmed the officer is "doing great" after Secret Service personnel intercepted the gunman charging the checkpoint.

In the immediate aftermath, social media users spread unverified claims the shooting was "staged," while influencer Debra Lea faced backlash for disputed security claims and posting a selfie shortly after the incident.

The incident prompted discussions about rescheduling the dinner and raised security questions at high-profile events, occurring nearly two years after Trump survived two separate assassination attempts.
title: Conspiracy Theories Flooded Social Media After WHCD Shooting

bias: Left-Leaning outlets amplify the biopic's triumph with hype like "juggernaut," "over-the-moon," and global $217 million totals—contrasting Straight Outta Compton's $60.2 million—while framing resilience via "shrugged off" for troubled production and reviews; right-leaning coverage de-emphasizes such elaboration, focusing minimally on core $97 million record.

summary: On Sunday, the Michael Jackson biopic 'Michael' launched with $97 million domestically and $217 million globally in its opening weekend, shattering records for music biopics.

Following a rocky production, Lionsgate spent up to $50 million on reshoots after discovering a legal settlement barred depicting 1993 child molestation accusations. Director Antoine Fuqua reworked the story to conclude in 1988.
Critics awarded the film only 38% positive on Rotten Tomatoes, yet audiences strongly disagreed, giving it an A- CinemaScore and 97% positive rating on Saturday.

The studio's advertising campaign tapped into nostalgia for Jackson's talent, while ticket sales for the Broadway stage musical 'MJ' have also increased. Projections suggest the $200 million production could exceed $700 million worldwide.
This success marks a significant turnaround for Lionsgate after 2024 flops including 'Borderlands' and 'White Bird,' with the studio expected to greenlight a sequel.
title: ‘Michael’ Moonwalks to $97 Million Opening, Shattering Record for Music Biopics

bias: Coverage splits sharply: left emphasizes victim-centered urgency and prescriptive language—using words like "should," "all," and "drunken driving" and foregrounding the child's amputation—framing alcohol-detection as a necessary safety fix; right stresses system-level alarm, highlighting phrases such as "not an isolated problem" and "troubling," offering shorter, less graphic accounts and treating the NTSB finding as grounds for broad action; center uses pragmatic verbs like "urges," and frames impacts with "face" and "possible," noting this is a first-time recommendation.
A key dividing line is victim-focused horror versus systemic prevalence; all sides nonetheless accept the NTSB recommendation and the West Virginia crash as the catalyst.
summary: On Thursday, the National Transportation Safety Board recommended that all new school buses be equipped with alcohol detection systems capable of disabling the vehicle if the driver appears impaired.
A 2020 Stateline report identified at least 118 school bus drivers ticketed or arrested for driving under the influence over five years, confirming impairment is not an isolated problem.

Peter Kurdock, general counsel for Advocates for Highway and Auto Safety, said the number of cases alarmed him. "Children going to and from the schoolhouse are America's most precious passengers," Kurdock said.

Kurdock predicted industry pushback from owners of the nation's half-million school buses. Federal regulators or states could require the technology, but Congress would need to pass legislation for widespread adoption.

Similar systems are already used on school buses in parts of Europe. This recommendation follows a previous NTSB push for alcohol detection in all new passenger vehicles, though that rule remains stuck in the rulemaking process.
title: NTSB Recommends Alcohol Detectors for New School Buses to Combat Impaired Driving
Trump says he speaks with Putin and Zelenskyy: Fox News

summary: On Sunday, President Donald Trump said Iran "can come to us, or they can call us" after cancelling planned negotiations in Islamabad on Saturday, citing excessive travel costs and an inadequate Iranian offer.

Talks appeared to collapse Saturday as Tehran's top diplomat left Pakistan, following a previous round in Islamabad where Vice President JD Vance and Iranian parliamentary speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf ended discussions without agreement.

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian told Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif that Tehran would not enter "imposed negotiations" under threats or blockade, while Trump deemed Iran's offer "not enough" to justify the 17-hour flight.

Two US Air Force C-17s carrying security staff and equipment departed Pakistan on Sunday according to Pakistani government sources, while Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi traveled to Oman to discuss regional security frameworks.

Trump reiterated that negotiators know "they cannot have a nuclear weapon, otherwise there's no reason to meet," while Iran has effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, disrupting global oil shipments and straining US-NATO relations.
title: Trump tells Fox News Iran can call US if it wants to negotiate

summary: University-Texarkana student Graycen Vargo died Friday after collapsing during a track competition at Xavier University in New Orleans . The junior suffered a medical emergency, received immediate onsite care, and was transported to a local hospital where he died.

Vargo transferred to the university this year from Jacksonville College in Jacksonville, Texas, and was a junior computer science major. He recently received the Eagle Excellence Award for his performance in Cross Country.

Athletics Director Ryan Wall conveyed the department's "heartfelt sorrow" to the Vargo family. The university confirmed student counseling services staff will remain available to assist students in need during this time.
title: Texas A&M-Texarkana runner dies after collapsing during competition in New Orleans

summary: On Friday night, Wellesley Police discovered two children dead inside a home on Edgemoor Avenue after responding to a well-being check request from a Vermont police department around 9:30 p.m.

Wellesley mother Janette MacAusland, 49, is scheduled for arraignment in Vermont Superior Court on Monday, facing a charge of fugitive from justice after being arrested and lodged at a Rutland facility.

The Norfolk District Attorney's Office and Massachusetts State Police are investigating while officials maintain there is no risk to the community; neighbors reported not seeing the children since Monday.

Neighbor David Kessler said police are seeking information about the children's father, stating, "They're wondering where the father is." District Attorney Michael Morrisey said more information is expected soon.
Wellesley, a suburb 15 miles west of Boston, is considered one of America's safest cities with 30,000 residents; Marjorie Freiman, chair of the town's select board, expressed sympathy to the family.
title: Massachusetts Police Investigate After 2 Children Found Dead in Wellesley Home

summary: Four candidates vying to lead the United Nations fielded questions from ambassadors this past week, seeking to distinguish themselves in a high-stakes selection process. Michelle Bachelet, Rafael Grossi, Rebeca Grynspan, and Macky Sall participated in the auditions.

The 15-nation Security Council will determine the next leader, with the 193-member General Assembly providing final approval. By tradition, the role rotates by region, and this year it is Latin America's turn.

Candidates faced varied scrutiny, with Bachelet responding to 28 Republican U.S. lawmakers who called her a "pro-abortion zealot." Sall, the only non-Latin American contender, faced demonstrations outside United Nations headquarters regarding corruption allegations he denies.

Contenders outlined their visions, with Grossi warning of "huge doubts" about the institution's effectiveness in solving global problems. Grynspan advocated for a "moral voice," while Sall pledged to be a "bridge-builder" to restore trust.

All four candidates pledged to reform the more than 80-year-old institution as the race intensifies. Minh-Thu Pham, CEO of the Starling Institute, noted a widespread desire for a leader willing to take risks.
title: 4 vying to be the next UN chief try to set themselves apart as race heats up

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Iran as a proactive "diplomatic initiator," using language like "fresh proposal" and "peace-making" to highlight a path toward "ending the war .
Conversely, right-leaning coverage emphasizes "skepticism" toward a "two-stage plan," portraying the offer as a tactic to bypass nuclear requirements.
While center-leaning reports focus on strategic "leverage" and internal Iranian "divisions," the left uniquely attributes the conflict to "joint U.S.-Israeli attacks.
Despite these ideological divides, all sides agree a proposal exists to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, reflecting a shared focus on maritime stability amidst deep-seated geopolitical distrust.
summary: On Sunday, Iran conveyed a proposal to the United States through Pakistani mediators to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and end the war, with nuclear negotiations postponed to a later stage, Axios reported.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi visited Oman for bilateral talks after meeting Pakistani officials in Islamabad, signaling Tehran's diplomatic push to engage regional allies during negotiations with Washington.
Tehran's proposal requires the United States to lift the blockade on Iranian assets crossing the Strait, which the government identified as a critical red line alongside nuclear matters.
President Donald Trump signaled on Sunday he plans to keep the blockade active until a complete deal is struck, telling Fox News, "I think they are under pressure."
The United States president further added that he believes the Iran war will come to an end "very soon," expressing confidence that Washington will emerge as the victor in the conflict.
title: Iran Offers U.S. Deal to Reopen Strait

bias: Right-Leaning outlets frame Senator Fetterman’s remarks as a heroic stand, using phrases like "last man standing" to portray him as a lone defender against a party "abandoning" Israel .
They employ charged language like "Hamas cheerleader" and "Nazi-linked" imagery to highlight internal rot, specifically naming individuals to anchor their critique.
Center-Leaning coverage similarly utilizes sensational language like "torches" and "surging" to describe the "ugly truth" but de-emphasizes specific names.
Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized.
Both perspectives converge on Fetterman’s blunt admission that a "problem" exists, reflecting a broader ideological divide over the party’s moral direction and foreign policy.
summary: On Saturday, Senator John Fetterman told CNN the Democratic Party "definitely" has a problem with antisemitism, describing support for Israel as becoming "toxic" within his party.

Fetterman pointed to Maine Democratic senatorial candidate Graham Platner, who leads primary polls despite controversy over a Nazi-linked tattoo and recently surfaced Reddit comments praising a 2014 Hamas raid.

The senator also criticized Democratic support for influencer Hasan Piker, who claimed "America deserved 9/11" and Hamas is "1,000 percent better" than Israel, while noting 80% of Democrats view Israel negatively.

During a Fox News appearance, Fetterman defended Israel's military actions against Hamas, Hezbollah, and Iran, admitting his pro-Israel stance is "politically toxic" within his party but remains committed.
title: Fetterman raises concerns about antisemitism within the Democratic Party

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the event as settler-inflicted violence—using charged words like "kills," "settler attack," "occupied West Bank," foregrounding the 14‑year‑old victim and allegations that forces "prevented... from reaching the school to rescue their children"—to emphasize state culpability and a pattern of escalation.
Right-Leaning pieces emphasize casualty counts and witness attribution with phrases like "witnesses say," de-emphasizing broader context or army actions.
Centers stress the army saying circumstances are "under review.
Both sides agree on two deaths, the 14‑year‑old, and health‑authority figures; the divide reflects deeper ideological and geopolitical framings.
summary: On Tuesday, Israeli settlers and soldiers opened fire in the West Bank village of al-Mughayyir, killing 14-year-old Aws al-Naasan and 32-year-old Jihad Abu Naim, witnesses said.
Located 9 miles northeast of Ramallah, al-Mughayyir has become an epicenter of clashes, with residents reporting dozens of attacks in 2025 including lethal shootings, arson, and vandalism.

Amin Abu Ulaya, head of the local council, said settlers attacked a school, creating panic as parents arrived amid gunfire and tear gas. "This led to the killing of a student and another person," he said.
Al-Naasan and Abu Naim are the latest Palestinians killed this year in the occupied West Bank, where 10 people have been killed by Israeli settlers, surpassing the 2025 total.
While some Israeli officials condemned "Jewish rioters" as a fringe minority, others have backed settler expansion, a practice the United Nations deems illegal under international law.
title: Two Palestinians killed during settler attack on West Bank village, officials say

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the saga as a "defeat" for the GOP and "key win" for Democrats, celebrating lower court rejections of Republican bids while touting potential four additional House seats from the narrowly approved 51%-49% map.

Right-Leaning coverage counters with sympathetic portrayals of Republicans "regrouping" for a "last-chance fight" and "holding out hope" for Supreme Court "intercession," emphasizing circuit court halts to certification over Democratic gains.
Pivotal divides emerge on procedural violations by the Democratic-led assembly and map's "favoring Democrats" legitimacy, with left legitimizing voter will and right questioning it.
Center-Leaning reports stay procedural, tracking lawsuits neutrally.
Amid partisan clashes, all note the Supreme Court hearing and narrow referendum, underscoring divides on electoral integrity versus democratic endorsement.
summary: The Virginia Supreme Court convenes Monday to hear oral arguments in Republican challenges to the redistricting referendum voters narrowly approved last week, which could determine whether a new congressional map takes effect.

Republican National Committee lawsuits allege the Democrat-controlled General Assembly exceeded its authority by failing to provide the required 90-day notice period before placing the constitutional amendment on the ballot.

Last Wednesday, Tazewell Circuit Judge Jack Hurley Jr. declared the ballot language unconstitutional and blocked certification, but the Supreme Court stayed his injunction, allowing the April 21 referendum to proceed pending review.

Attorney General Jay Jones is defending the referendum, arguing a pause preserves both sides' arguments while the court determines whether the new congressional map stands and potentially shifts U.S. House seats.
Virginia Democrats celebrate the narrow 51%-49% victory as the will of voters, yet a definitive court decision is critical before the May 26 candidate filing deadline for November midterm elections.
title: Virginia Supreme Court considers whether to block voter-approved US House map favoring Democrats

bias: Left-Leaning coverage employs skeptical qualifiers like "says" to frame Trump's announcement amid tensions, casting doubt on its certainty.

Right-Leaning outlets counter with authoritative "confirms" and resilient phrasing like "despite shooting incident," while highlighting threats via "gunman stormed" to underscore conservative fortitude against violence.

Pivotal divides emerge on the shooter's anti-Trump manifesto, emphasized only by the right, and security ramp-ups, noted neutrally by center-leaning reports attributing Trump's "brave" praise without spin.
Both sides agree the visit proceeds, with Trump hailing the monarch's bravery, exposing ideological rifts on threat resilience versus diplomatic skepticism.
summary: President Donald Trump confirmed on Sunday that British King Charles III will proceed with his planned four-day state visit to the United States, marking the 250th anniversary of American independence.

Security concerns emerged after a lone gunman attempted to storm the White House Correspondents' Dinner on Saturday, prompting Secret Service agents to rush President Trump and Vice President JD Vance to safety.
Trump praised the "very brave" King on Sunday, telling Fox News the monarch is "a fantastic person and a tremendous representative" of his country who will have a "great time" during the visit.

Buckingham Palace is holding talks with US authorities to determine if Saturday's events require operational changes, as security experts warn of risks regarding the King becoming "collateral damage" during future attempts on Trump.

Prime Minister Keir Starmer's government hopes the visit will reinforce the US-UK "special relationship" despite recent diplomatic strains, with senior minister Darren Jones confirming security services remain in close cooperation for the upcoming trip.
title: Trump hails ‘brave’ King Charles for US visit after shooting

summary: On Saturday, April 25, 2026, a gunman fired shots at the White House Correspondents' Dinner, forcing the immediate evacuation of President Donald Trump and other officials. The event, attended by more than 2,500 guests, was canceled following the security breach.

Following a shootout with Secret Service agents at a security checkpoint, authorities apprehended the suspect, 31-year-old Cole Tomas Allen. The California resident faces charges including assault on a federal officer using a dangerous weapon.

White House Correspondents' Association President Weijia Jiang, seated beside Trump at the dais, managed evacuation protocols before asking the first question at the president's post-shooting press briefing. Colleagues praised her composure throughout the chaotic evening.
Trump praised law enforcement for their swift intervention, stating he refuses to let "thugs" disrupt the fabric of American life. He vowed to reschedule the dinner within 30 days.
The suspect's arraignment is set for Monday, where additional charges are expected. The White House Correspondents' Association board is meeting to assess the incident and determine the path forward.
title: CBS journalist Weijia Jiang gets props for poise under pressure after press dinner shooting

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame this tragedy through public service, using reverential language like "honor" to eulogize the victim’s legacy.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage employs sensationalist rhetoric, describing a "fireball" event while explicitly tagging the victim as a "Democrat Lawmaker" to foreground partisan identity.
While the right highlights the victim’s age of 67, center-leaning reports maintain a clinical tone, focusing on "authorities confirm.
A key divide exists in legislative legacy; the left highlights specific policy work, while the right de-emphasizes it.
Both sides agree on the two fatalities and the necessity of an NTSB investigation.
summary: On Saturday, April 25, 2026, a Beech F33A aircraft crashed into Southbrook Park in Brooklyn Park, Minnesota, shortly after departing Crystal Airport, killing both occupants: North Dakota State Representative Liz Conmy and pilot Joe Cass.

Conmy, a member of the North Dakota Democratic Nonpartisan League representing the 11th District, was serving her first term after election in 2022. Governor Kelly Armstrong ordered state and U.S. flags flown at half-staff to honor her.

The National Transportation Safety Board and Federal Aviation Administration are investigating the crash. Witnesses described hearing a 'loud boom' followed by massive flames erupting in the park, with fire crews extinguishing the blaze within minutes.

Conmy's death creates a vacancy in the North Dakota House District 11, disrupting representation and pending committee work. Colleagues remembered her as a champion for public education, environmental stewardship, and transparent elections.

Crystal Airport handles at least 40,000 operations annually, heightening local scrutiny of aviation safety. The crash follows a March 2025 incident in Brooklyn Park that killed 63-year-old U.S. Bank executive Terry Dolan.
title: A small plane crash near Minneapolis kills North Dakota state legislator and the pilot

bias: Left-Leaning coverage amplifies "threat" and "at risk" with heavy repetition, framing light pollution as an existential peril to the Atacama's "darkest skies," evoking urgent environmental advocacy, while right-leaning outlets de-emphasize it via truncation and low frequency, omitting skepticism toward overregulation or economic trade-offs.
Left employs alarmist, adversarial tones personifying pollution as aggressive, contrasting right's restrained neutrality.
Pivotal divides emerge on astronomical projects like the Extremely Large Telescope, highlighted by both extremes but absent in center, which adds "driest place" details for vivid stakes.
All agree on clearest views endangered, underscoring preservationist consensus amid development divides.
summary: In January, an energy firm canceled a project near Paranal Observatory following massive scientific appeals, but experts warn the Atacama Desert remains vulnerable due to lax, outdated sky preservation laws.

Eduardo Unda-Sanzana, director of the Astronomy Center at the University of Antofagasta, notes that industrial development and mining threaten the "ocean of darkness" that astronomers have relied on for decades.

The European Southern Observatory is building the $1.5 billion Extremely Large Telescope at Paranal, which will be 20 times more powerful than today's leading telescopes and 15 times sharper than NASA's Hubble Space Telescope.

Daniela González, director of the Cielos de Chile Foundation, is working to ensure new government criteria strictly protect astronomical areas, following recent ministerial advisory commission recommendations to Chile.

Without updated regulations, similar projects could be proposed at any moment, and unchecked industrial impacts like light pollution and atmospheric turbulence could render astronomical activities unviable.
title: Chile's Dark Skies Remain at Risk Despite Energy Project Cancellation, Scientists Warn

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize repeated "layers of failure" and "lax" protocols in Trump's protection, framing incidents like agents allegedly forgetting Melania as indictments of incompetence, while right-leaning ones spotlight the Secret Service's outright "failed" duty to Trump, portraying him as a victim of institutional negligence amid his praise for responders.
Left employs dramatic condemnation with terms like "greatest mistake," evoking outrage at leadership lapses; right uses stark accusations to stoke agency distrust.
Pivotal divides emerge over family oversights versus Trump commendations.
Center coverage balances scrutiny with "massive security success" and bipartisan notes.
All agree on the shooting's occurrence and protocol questions, underscoring ideological rifts on federal reliability versus political vulnerability.
summary: On Saturday night, a gunman opened fire at the Washington Hilton during the White House Correspondents' Association dinner, wounding a Secret Service agent and prompting immediate evacuation of President Donald Trump and other officials.

Washington police reported the alleged gunman, Cole Tomas Allen, was staying at the Washington Hilton and reached a floor above the ballroom armed with a shotgun, handgun, and knives, breaching security despite protocols for roughly 2,600 attendees.

Secret Service agents quickly rushed Trump and Vice President Vance from the head table, while security details for Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent formed human shields to protect them during the evacuation.

President Trump later described the Washington Hilton as "not particularly a secure building," noting the suspect had not been close to "breaching" the ballroom. The venue was previously the site of a 1981 assassination attempt against President Ronald Reagan.

Coming less than two years after assassination attempts during the 2024 campaign, Saturday's incident raises questions about protection afforded to America's political leaders. Investigators are currently reviewing security gaps that allowed the gunman to access the hotel.
title: Trump's security again faces scrutiny after press dinner shooting

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the shooting as tied to "Trump Dinner" peril and mock "MAGA" reactions as monolithic, using derisive tones like "crazy," "evil," and "sick guy" to downplay ideological motives while highlighting family warnings.
Right-Leaning coverage portrays the suspect as a "wannabe Trump assassin" with "radical ties," emphasizing anti-Christian manifesto details and "No Kings" protests to evoke conservative victimhood.
Center-Leaning reports stay neutral with attributed "likely" targeting claims and timelines.
Pivotal divides emerge over suspect's trans gun advice versus prior alerts .
All agree on Acting AG Blanche's targeting statement and no serious injuries, underscoring partisan lenses on political violence amid cultural rifts.
summary: On Saturday, April 25, 2026, a gunman attempted to breach the White House Correspondents' Dinner at the Washington Hilton, forcing President Donald Trump 's immediate evacuation. Secret Service agents tackled suspect Cole Tomas Allen near the ballroom as gunfire erupted.

Federal authorities identified the 31-year-old suspect as Cole Tomas Allen of Torrance, California, who allegedly traveled by train to Washington, D.C. Writings recovered by officials suggest Allen intended to target administration members, though he is not cooperating with investigators.

Acting U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro announced Allen faces imminent federal charges including firearm offenses and assault on a federal officer. Armed with a shotgun, handgun, and knives, Allen shot one Secret Service officer, who survived due to protective gear.

World leaders including Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi condemned the violence and praised security's swift response. President Trump vowed the dinner will be rescheduled within 30 days, while officials affirmed layered security measures prevented the gunman from reaching the ballroom.

Investigators are analyzing a manifesto and electronic devices to determine Allen's full motive and any broader threat. The incident at the Washington Hilton echoes the 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan at the same venue, reigniting security protocol discussions.
title: Accused gunman at correspondents’ dinner was believed to be targeting top officials, Blanche says

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Tucker Carlson 's rift as a personal "betrayed" "breakup," emphasizing emotional "regret" and a tense final meeting to highlight Trump's insincerity and conservative disunity, while right-leaning coverage portrays it as Trump becoming a "slave" to "neocons," violating non-intervention promises and underscoring Carlson's stature as a key re-election ally.
Left employs dramatic victimhood tones like "valid question" on complicity; right uses pejorative hawk critiques.
Center softens with "captured" by neocons.
Pivotal divides emerge over Iran war direction and neocon influence.
All agree on Carlson's betrayal sentiment and policy critique, reflecting isolationist versus interventionist ideological tensions.
summary: Political commentator Tucker Carlson expressed feeling "betrayed" by President Donald Trump in a Saturday interview with The Wall Street Journal, citing aggressive military actions in the Middle East as a departure from his "America First" campaign promises.

Carlson, a former informal adviser to Trump, points to the February 28 U.S. and Israeli airstrikes that killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei as the breaking point that deeply divided conservatives expecting an anti-war stance.
Trump labeled Carlson a "low IQ person" earlier this month, while Carlson responded by calling the president a "slave" to external forces and apologizing for "misleading" voters in 2024.

Positioning himself as the "most prominent opponent of the Iran war," Carlson argued that foreign conflicts are a "generational problem that didn't start with Trump," suggesting the system ultimately proved stronger than the president.
title: Tucker Carlson Says He Feels ‘Betrayed’ by Trump Over Iran War: WSJ
